what_it_does: Defines the connector vocabulary for a movement layer, including stroke styling, terminal forms, movement verbs, seam behavior, factory mapping, and related annotation/value/pointer rules.
observations: Metadata frames this as connectors-3 and says it adds the ORBIT verb plus three corpus-direct connector forms.
Stroke style uses a 1.5 width, bronze line color, and unit-derived dash/gap sizing.
Terminals distinguish socket, plug, and arrowhead, with gold used at arrival or filled terminal emphasis.
Movement verbs include transport, gather, descend, and orbit, each with descriptive behavior and evidence references.
Gather encodes direction as in/out, with a default of in.
Seam law restricts boundary-crossing elements to this connector layer.
Annotation arrows, value flow, and mirror law are described outside the verbs object as related connector-facing rules.
standouts: Direction is treated as a property of a verb rather than a separate verb.
The file separates moving dashed connector behavior from solid structural hairlines.
Mirror law defines pointer orientation by referent position.
Value flow records a retired green variant and current gold treatment.
conventions: Records are descriptive JSON with prose fields rather than executable schema declarations.
Evidence fields cite deck slides, variants, and corpus-direct harvest references.
Design tokens are referenced with brace syntax such as {color.accent.bronze_line}.
Verb entries mix shared fields like does, geometry, direction, evidence with direction-specific meanings.
Some adjacent rules use is/evidence instead of the verb does/direction structure.
concerns: factory_mapping lists transport, gather(in|out), and descend but does not include orbit, while meta says connectors-3 adds ORBIT.
The direction field has mixed shapes: objects for most verbs and a string for orbit.
annotation_arrow, value_flow, and mirror_law appear connector-related but are not represented as verbs or as a separate typed collection.
notes: Brief this as the movement-connector vocabulary file: it names connector behaviors, visual marks, boundary rules, and evidence-backed design laws.
questions: Should orbit be included in factory_mapping?
Are annotation_arrow, value_flow, and mirror_law intended to compile to the same factory component as movement verbs?
Is the mixed direction shape intentional or an artifact of prose-first authoring?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the file: it defines connector movement vocabulary and related connector-layer laws, with some additions sitting outside the core verbs collection.
intention_signals: It seems to be trying to preserve a compact, evidence-linked vocabulary for how visual relationships move, converge, descend, orbit, point, and cross seams in layouts.
novelty: The file treats visual connector behavior like a grammar of verbs with directional properties, while also capturing editorial arrows, value badges, and pointer mirroring as ledgered movement-adjacent laws.
contribution: Contributes a neutral movement layer vocabulary: connector verbs, terminal styling, seam-crossing rules, annotation/value-flow patterns, mirror orientation behavior, and evidence references for preserving these relationships in a fused system.
summary_for_embedding: This JSON file defines the counterpart-design connector layer: stroke and terminal styling, movement verbs, seam law, factory mapping, annotation arrows, value flow, and mirror-orientation rules with evidence references.
intention_for_embedding: It was made to encode the visual grammar of movement connectors so layouts and factory components can share a consistent vocabulary for relationships and boundary-crossing marks.
